title: ALTA title commitment structure
text: ALTA title commitment has 3 schedules: Schedule A (effective date, proposed insured, amount, estate type, vesting); Schedule B-I (requirements — must be satisfied before policy issues, e.g. payoff existing mortgage); Schedule B-II (exceptions to coverage — appear as exclusions in the final policy, e.g. easements / restrictive covenants / unrecorded items).

title: ASTM E1527-21 Phase I ESA
text: Phase I Environmental Site Assessment standard. Required for CERCLA innocent landowner defense. Components: records review (federal/state/tribal/local databases), site reconnaissance, interviews, historical-use research, identification of RECs (Recognized Environmental Conditions), Controlled RECs (CRECs), Historical RECs (HRECs), de minimis conditions. Valid for 1 year (with some materials valid for 180 days).

title: ASTM E1903-19 Phase II ESA
text: Phase II Environmental Site Assessment standard. Subsurface investigation when Phase I identifies RECs. Components: sampling plan, soil + groundwater + vapor sampling, laboratory analysis, data interpretation, recommendations for further action or no-further-action (NFA).

title: IBC structural code summary
text: International Building Code Chapters 16 (Structural Design), 17 (Special Inspections), 18 (Soils + Foundations), 19 (Concrete), 20 (Aluminum), 21 (Masonry), 22 (Steel), 23 (Wood). Local jurisdictions adopt with amendments — check the specific adoption ordinance.

title: Typical zoning code structure
text: Zoning codes typically address: (1) use districts (R-1 residential / C-1 commercial / M-1 manufacturing / etc.); (2) bulk/density regulations (FAR, lot coverage, setbacks); (3) height limits; (4) parking minimums; (5) signage; (6) variance + special-use-permit procedures; (7) nonconforming use protections; (8) overlay districts (historic / flood / TOD).

title: ALTA/NSPS Land Title Survey
text: Standardized survey for commercial real-estate transactions. Required Table A items selected by buyer. Identifies: boundaries, easements, encroachments, access, utilities, building locations, flood zones, parking counts, ADA paths. Becomes part of the title insurance policy.

title: Certificate of Occupancy
text: Local building department issues CO certifying that a structure complies with building codes for the intended use. Required for new buildings, change of use, substantial alterations. Operating a property without a valid CO can trigger penalties, lien on rents, and inability to transfer.

title: Title exceptions / encumbrances
text: Common title exceptions: easements (utility / access / scenic / conservation), restrictive covenants (CCRs), encroachments (neighboring improvements over the line), mineral rights reservations, oil and gas leases, mechanics liens, judgments / tax liens, mortgages, leases of record, eminent domain proceedings.

title: Mechanics lien priority + cure
text: Contractor / subcontractor / supplier can record a lien against the property for unpaid work. Priority typically relates back to commencement of work (NOT date of recording in many states). Cure: pay + obtain release-of-lien filing. Uncured liens can prevent transfer or be insured-around with title-insurance company endorsement.

title: REC vs CREC vs HREC vs de minimis
text: REC = current presence or likely past presence of hazardous substance / petroleum that has resulted in / is likely to result in release. CREC = REC where regulatory closure was conditional on activity/use limitations. HREC = past contamination addressed to applicable regulatory standards with no residual restrictions. De minimis = not subject to enforcement, no investigation required.
